name: Second Harvest Food Bank
mission: Summer Meals Second Harvest works with school districts and community partners to maximize the use of federally funded school meals.
theoryOfChange: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley has a published theory of change for its 'One Community Initiative'. This theory of change aims to understand the program's impact on client experiences and, in turn, food insecurity and hunger outcomes. The strategy also includes a prioritized set of outcome measures, a design for an internally led evaluation of the 'Free Community Markets' pilot, and a learning agenda to guide future evaluation of the 'One Community Initiative'.

scoreSummary: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley shows exceptional impact and good alignment (minor risk deductions applied).

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Good donor alignment with manageable risk. Evidence quality is solid but still evolving in places.

keyConcerns.headline: 74% of reported revenue is noncash (gifts-in-kind)
keyConcerns.detail: Noncash contributions inflate reported program ratios. Cash-adjusted metrics better reflect actual spending.

components.improvement_suggestion: Add a dedicated zakat page or clearly state whether donations are zakat-eligible.

judge_issues.message: Website shows related EIN (94-2581445), likely parent/subsidiary of 94-2614101
BBB name 'Second Harvest Food Bank of North Central Ohio' may not match 'Second Harvest Food Bank' (similarity: 57%)
Evaluation source domain 'fconline.foundationcenter.org' is not a known trusted evaluator
Website citation for 'claims_zakat_eligible' uses homepage URL; deep link preferred

risks.risk_summary: Key concern: GIK inflation: 74% of revenue is noncash contributions

risks.description: GIK inflation: 74% of revenue is noncash contributions

score_details.score_summary: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley shows exceptional impact and good alignment (minor risk deductions applied).

zakat.claim_evidence: No explicit mention of 'zakat' acceptance or rejection on their website or donation pages was found in the search results. The organization focuses on providing food assistance to those in need in Santa Clara and San Mateo counties. (Source: https://www.shfb.org)

all_citations.claim: The organization holds a 100.100.0/100 from Charity Navigator score and a 94.8% program expense ratio.
Total revenue for the period was $286,205,684.
The organization works with school districts to maximize federally funded meals and manages significant food rescue operations.
The charity maintains high program service expenses and efficient fundraising metrics.
The organization serves a high volume of average monthly clients through its network.
The organization offers various donation methods for general charitable giving and sadaqah.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: The organization earns a 69/100 due to its impeccable financial transparency and direct service model, though the high cost of operating in Silicon Valley impacts its overall efficiency score. It is a reliable choice for sadaqah, offering high data confidence and a proven track record.
areas_for_improvement: High cost per beneficiary relative to global hunger relief benchmarks
Moderate alignment for donors seeking specific religious-based giving frameworks

dimension_explanations.alignment: Second Harvest addresses the extreme poverty and food insecurity present in one of the nation's most expensive regions [3]. While the organization is sadaqah-eligible and offers various ways to give, it maintains a medium donor fit for those specifically seeking Islamic-centered governance, as it is a secular community-based organization [6].
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization demonstrates high impact through a direct service model that rescues food and provides groceries to an average of hundreds of thousands of clients monthly [3][5]. With a program expense ratio of 94.8%, nearly all revenue is directed toward core services like the Food Connection and Grocery Programs [1][2]. However, the cost per beneficiary is approximately $528.78, reflecting the high-cost environment of its service area [3].

baseline_narrative.headline: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley provides critical food security to Northern California through high-efficiency grocery programs and federal meal partnerships.
strengths: Exceptional financial health with a perfect Charity Navigator score of 100.0/100
Highly efficient fundraising operations costing only $0.03 per $1 raised
Strong liquidity with 9.5 months of working capital available
baseline_narrative.summary: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley operates as a major food bank network, leveraging school districts and community partners to maximize federally funded summer meals [3]. The organization maintains a high level of operational efficiency, reporting total revenue of $286,205,684 while ensuring that the vast majority of funds directly support hunger relief efforts [2][4].

impact.improvement: To improve impact scores, the organization could work to <cite id="16">reduce the cost per beneficiary</cite> through further logistical optimizations.

rich_narrative.headline: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley provides critical food security to Northern California through high-efficiency grocery programs and federal meal partnerships.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who prioritize maximum financial efficiency and want to support large-scale, professionally managed hunger relief in Northern California.
donor_motivations: Value a perfect 100.0/100 from Charity Navigator rating
Want to ensure $0.94 of every dollar goes to programs
Care about local food insecurity in the Silicon Valley region
Prefer organizations with deep institutional roots (founded 1974)
giving_considerations: Donations are sadaqah-eligible but not verified for zakat
The organization is a large institution, not a small grassroots charity
High reliance on food donations means cash is used for critical logistics
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking international impact or those specifically looking for zakat-verified pathways.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade A due to <cite id="21">4 outcome metrics tracked</cite> and <cite id="22">2 external evaluation documents</cite>.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: SHSV transitions from crisis response to sustainable community work by maximizing federal meal programs and rescuing food waste.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that the 94 million meals provided actually reach those in need efficiently.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: While revenue has dipped, the high-cost environment of Silicon Valley ensures ongoing demand for food services.

organizational_capacity.geographic_reach: Santa Clara and San Mateo Counties

peer_comparison.differentiator: SHSV outperforms the <cite id="20">peer program ratio median of 89.8%</cite> with its <cite id="4">94.6% efficiency</cite>.

similar_organizations.differentiator: Global emergency response vs local focus
similar_organizations.name: World Central Kitchen

strengths.detail: The organization maintains a <cite id="4">94.8% program expense ratio</cite> and an incredibly low fundraising cost of <cite id="8">$0.03 per $1 raised</cite>.
SHSV demonstrates high financial resilience with <cite id="9">9.5 months of working capital</cite> available to sustain operations during economic shifts.
The charity holds a <cite id="10">Platinum Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite> and meets <cite id="11">all 20 BBB Wise Giving Alliance standards</cite>.

strengths_deep_dive: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley's strength lies in its massive scale and logistical precision. By managing <cite id="12">$242,425,070 in total assets</cite>, the organization can absorb and distribute large-scale donations efficiently. Their governance is equally strong, featuring an <cite id="13">independent board of 17 members</cite> with a <cite id="14">94% independence rate</cite>, ensuring rigorous oversight of their <cite id="3">$286 million annual budget</cite>.
rich_narrative.summary: Second Harvest of Silicon Valley (SHSV) is a premier hunger-relief organization serving Northern California, specifically Santa Clara and San Mateo counties. Founded in <cite id="1">1974</cite>, the organization has evolved into a massive logistics and distribution hub that provided <cite id="2">94 million meals</cite> in the most recent fiscal year. SHSV operates through a highly efficient model, leveraging <cite id="3">$286,205,684 in total revenue</cite> to combat food insecurity in one of the nation's most expensive regions. The charity maintains a <cite id="4">94.8% program expense ratio</cite>, ensuring that the vast majority of its resources are directed toward its core mission of grocery programs, CalFresh outreach, and school meal partnerships. Its operational excellence is further evidenced by a <cite id="5">perfect 100/100 overall score from Charity Navigator</cite>, a distinction it has held for <cite id="6">16 consecutive years</cite>. Beyond direct food distribution, SHSV focuses on sustainability, having <cite id="7">rescued 84 million pounds of food</cite> to prevent waste while feeding the hungry. While the organization operates on a large scale, it maintains lean fundraising operations, spending only <cite id="8">$0.03 to raise every dollar</cite>.
